metadata.title: Meta-Learner Showdown: Who Estimates CATE Best?
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: S-learner, T-learner, X-learner — same data, wildly different CATE estimates. Which do you trust and why?
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'three meta-learners, one dataset, different answers'; the question of which to trust

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Build a Python comparison of S-learner, T-learner, and X-learner on synthetic data with known heterogeneous effects. Print MAE vs ground truth by age quartile.

props.command: claude "Write a Python meta-learner comparison: synthetic n=3000, feature age (18-65), binary treatment T, outcome Y = 2 + 0.3*age*(T==1) + noise. Fit: (1) S-learner: one RandomForest with T as feature; (2) T-learner: two separate RandomForests; (3) X-learner: propensity-weighted residual. Print MAE vs true CATE by age quartile. No causalml library."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The X-learner's key innovation: impute counterfactual outcomes using the other group's model, then regress the imputed treatment effect. This corrects for imbalance the T-learner ignores.

props.code:
# meta_learner_compare.py — Claude Code output

# X-learner stage 1: T-learner fits
m1.fit(X[T==1], y[T==1])
m0.fit(X[T==0], y[T==0])

# X-learner stage 2: impute counterfactuals
tau_t = y[T==1] - m0.predict(X[T==1])  # treated group
tau_c = m1.predict(X[T==0]) - y[T==0]  # control group

# X-learner stage 3: weighted combination
propensity = LogReg().fit(X, T).predict_proba(X)[:,1]
cate = propensity * mx0.predict(X) + (1-propensity) * mx1.predict(X)

beats.narration_text: The grouped bar chart shows MAE by age quartile. The S-learner is worst in older quartiles — it can't isolate the treatment indicator from confounders. X-learner cuts error by 66 percent vs S-learner.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: grouped bar chart MAE by age quartile for 3 meta-learners

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add cross-fitting to the X-learner — split the sample so the imputed outcomes come from out-of-fold predictions. This removes overfitting bias from stage 2.

props.command:
claude "update meta_learner_compare.py:
  -> add 5-fold cross-fitting to X-learner stages 1 and 2
  -> compare cross-fit vs naive X-learner MAE
  -> show variance reduction from cross-fitting"

beats.narration_text: Cross-fitting reduces X-learner MAE by another 15 percent. The key: each fold's counterfactual imputations come from models that never saw those observations during training — eliminating in-sample bias.
beats.visual_intent: slate: cross-fit vs naive X-learner MAE comparison; fold-level variance breakdown

beats.narration_text: The right meta-learner depends on three things: sample size, treatment assignment balance, and where heterogeneity lives. Small treated group? X-learner. Balanced and large? T-learner is fine. Single model fits? S-learner.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: decision tree for choosing S/T/X learner

beats.narration_text: Your move: run all three on your last A/B test. If the X-learner substantially outperforms the T-learner, you have imbalanced treatment assignment. That imbalance is also a propensity problem — the next reel covers propensity score weighting.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run comparison, check imbalance signal, proceed to propensity reel

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
